summary:
The Irish War of Independence (1919–1921) was a guerrilla war fought between the Irish Republican Army (IRA) and British forces, principally the Royal Irish Constabulary (RIC) and auxiliary paramilitary units. The conflict arose from Ireland's January 1919 declaration of independence by the First Dáil (Irish parliament) following the 1918 general election in which Sinn Féin won an overwhelming majority. IRA units under the strategic direction of Michael Collins conducted ambushes and targeted killings of intelligence personnel, while British forces responded with reprisal attacks on civilian populations.

The war was characterized by its asymmetric nature. The IRA, never numbering more than a few thousand active fighters, successfully disrupted British administration through selective violence and intelligence operations rather than conventional battle. British counterinsurgency measures, particularly the actions of the Black and Tans and Auxiliaries, caused significant civilian casualties and generated both domestic and international outrage. A ceasefire was declared in July 1921.

Negotiations in London produced the Anglo-Irish Treaty of December 1921, which established the Irish Free State as a self-governing dominion rather than an independent republic. Six counties of Ulster remained within the United Kingdom as Northern Ireland. The treaty split Irish nationalism, leading to the [[event:2721|Irish Civil War]] (1922–1923) and leaving the partition of Ireland as an enduring political problem addressed in part by the [[event:2721|Good Friday Agreement]] of 1998.
